coredata.dc:title: Sandwich-like electrode with tungsten nitride nanosheets decorated with carbon dots as efficient electrocatalyst for oxygen reduction
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Jie
Chen, Jinwei
Luo, Yan
Chen, Yihan
Wei, Xiaoyang
Wang, Gang
Wang, Ruilin
coredata.dc:description: Developing efficient and cost-effective oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) catalysts for fuel cells is quite necessary but still full of challenges. In this study, tungsten nitride nanosheets (WN NSs) decorated with nitrogen-containing carbon dots hybrid materials are developed using a facile two-step route. Specifically, a sandwich-like porous electrode, which contains a N-doped carbon layer encapsulated with uniform carbon dots (N-CDs) and a sheet-like WN for both-side formation of N-CDs, is obtained and designed as a novel type of ORR catalysts (denoted as N-CDs@WN). The N-CDs@WN hybrid shows superior catalytic activity via a four-electron pathway and demonstrates excellent stability over long periods of time and resistance to methanol. The excellent electrocatalytic properties of N-CDs@WN hybrids benefited from a hierarchical structure with large specific area, numerous active sites, and synergistic effect between CDs and WN that can facilitate the conductivity and mass transfer during the ORR process. Compared to other carbon dots-based nanocomposites, our facile synthetic route for N-CDs@WN not only presents the advantages of being relatively green, simple, and cost-effective but also is an attractive alternative for the electrocatalyst in the non-precious ORR.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Sandwich-like
Carbon dot
Tungsten nitride
Nanosheets
Oxygen reduction reaction

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The work was supported by the Key Research and Development Projects in Sichuan Province ( 2017GZ0397 ), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 51602209 ), the Provincial Nature Science Foundation of Sichuan (Nos. 2016GZ0423 , 2017CC0017 , 2018FZ0105 ) and the Fundamental Research Funds supported by Ministry of Education of the People’s Republic of China (No. YJ201746 , 2018SCUH0025 ). In addition, Jie Zhang would wish to give her sincere thanks to Nako Yabuki and Jennie Tang for their great support and tremendous encouragement.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the synthesis of sandwich-like N-CDs@WN.

(a) The particle number distribution of resulting CDs, (b) XRD patterns of the CDs, WN NSs, and the synthesized N-CDs@WN hybrid, (c) Raman spectra of CDs and N-CDs, (d) Raman spectra of N-CDs@WN hybrid.
Adsorption–desorption measurements of the CDs, WN, N-CDs, and N-CDs@WN hybrids. (a) N2 adsorption isotherms and (b) pore size distribution.
TEM images of the as-prepared (a) CDs, (b) N-CDs, (c) SEM image and (d) TEM image of pristine WN NSs, (e) TEM image and (f) HRTEM image of N-CDs@WN hybrid.
(a) XPS survey spectrum of N-CDs@WN hybrid, (b) C1s spectra, (c) W4f spectra, and (d) N1s spectra.

(a) LSV curves CDs, WN, N-CDs, CD + WN mixture, N-CDs@WN, and commercial Pt/C in O2-saturated 0.1 M KOH solution at a rotation speed of 1600 rpm and a scan rate of 10 mV s−1; (b) LSV curves of N-CDs@WN at different rotation speeds (625–2025 rpm), inset is K-L plots obtained from at the given potentials; (c) kinetic current density on the potential for CDs, WN, N-CDs, CD + WN mixture, N-CDs@WN, and Pt/C in the O2-saturated 0.1 M KOH; (d) Tafel plots for the as-prepared N-CDs@WN, compared with the Pt/C.

(a) Current–time (i–t) chronoamperometric responses of N-CDs@WN and Pt/C at 0.7 V in O2-saturated 0.1 M KOH at a rotation rate of 1600 rpm; (b) i–t responses of N-CDs@WN and Pt/C upon the introduction of 3 M methanol.
Comparison results of carbon dots-based ORR catalysts.

head.ce:title: Sandwich-like electrode with tungsten nitride nanosheets decorated with carbon dots as efficient electrocatalyst for oxygen reduction

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Materials Science and Engineering, Sichuan University, Chengdu 610065, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Developing efficient and cost-effective oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) catalysts for fuel cells is quite necessary but still full of challenges. In this study, tungsten nitride nanosheets (WN NSs) decorated with nitrogen-containing carbon dots hybrid materials are developed using a facile two-step route. Specifically, a sandwich-like porous electrode, which contains a N-doped carbon layer encapsulated with uniform carbon dots (N-CDs) and a sheet-like WN for both-side formation of N-CDs, is obtained and designed as a novel type of ORR catalysts (denoted as N-CDs@WN). The N-CDs@WN hybrid shows superior catalytic activity via a four-electron pathway and demonstrates excellent stability over long periods of time and resistance to methanol. The excellent electrocatalytic properties of N-CDs@WN hybrids benefited from a hierarchical structure with large specific area, numerous active sites, and synergistic effect between CDs and WN that can facilitate the conductivity and mass transfer during the ORR process. Compared to other carbon dots-based nanocomposites, our facile synthetic route for N-CDs@WN not only presents the advantages of being relatively green, simple, and cost-effective but also is an attractive alternative for the electrocatalyst in the non-precious ORR.

ce:section.ce:para: Proton exchange membrane fuel cells (PEMFCs) are thought to be promising energy conversion systems for stationary and mobile devices due to their numerous advantages such as quick operability at low temperatures, no electrolyte decomposition, high power density, and zero-pollution [1]. However, their efficiency is largely restricted by the sluggish cathode kinetics of the oxygen reduction reaction (ORR), and this hinders the widespread commercialization [2]. Currently, platinum (Pt) and its alloy nanoparticles with high surface area have proven to be robust electrocatalysts for ORR in terms of activity and stability [3–5]. However, the high costs, inadequate reserves, and inferior durability caused by poisoning still are the main impediments to extensive industrial applications of PEMFCs [6,7]. For these reasons, the Pt-based electrocatalysts need to be replaced with ORR electrocatalysts with improved durability and activity which are made from low-cost and earth-abundant materials. In the last decade, non-precious-metal catalysts (NPMCs) with good electrocatalytic performances for ORR have attracted increasing interest as a substitution for Pt.

With advancements in NPMCs, several transition metal-based ORR catalysts have been investigated, including transition metal dichalcogenides, oxides, carbides, and nitrides [8–13]. Among them, transition metal nitrides (TMNs) are regarded as promising substitutes for Pt, because of its Pt-like electronic behavior, originating from the changes in the lattice parameter and electronic property caused by incorporated N atoms [11,14]. It is proposed that TMNs will facilitate the O2 adsorption and dissociation. However, problems arising from facet control, due to meticulous high-temperature nitridation syntheses and inadequate anisotropy [15], have been a hindrance to both large-scale production and developing practical applications. To solve these problems, previous research has shown that nitride nanostructures (i.e. molybdenum nitride nanobelts [16], VN hollow spheres [17], and alloyed Co-Mo nitride [18], etc.) with unique facet exposure, specific structures and even dispersion can be obtained through a novel approach resulting in specific morphologies. Among various types of TMNs, tungsten nitrides (WN) have attracted considerable attention for their significant advantages, such as electrochemical stability and resistance to corrosion. Also, WN can perform as an ORR catalyst [10,19–21]. Specifically, the construction of nanosheets (NSs), nanoparticles (NPs), and nanowires (NWs) can enhance the electrocatalytic activities of nanostructures due to the increased exposure of active sites [22]. However, there has been few investigations in developing an optimized synthesis of the WN nanostructures. It is necessary to control the crystal growth of WN nanostructures to preserve electrochemical stability while enhancing ORR activity. Furthermore, WN modified with carbon materials is a relatively new research area which remains ambiguous for the ORR.

On the other hand, nitrogen-doped carbon materials, such as carbon nanofiber, porous carbon, N-doped carbon nanotube and graphene nanosheets have been extensively studied by properly designed structures and well controlled preparation with promising ORR performances [23–26]. Very recently, as a novel zero-dimensional (0D) carbon nanomaterial, carbon dots (CDs) have also been proposed as having potential for fabrication and design of energy storage devices due to their extraordinary properties and functions, such as their high surface area and sufficient electrochemical properties [27]. Shen et al. [28] synthesized unique carbon–nitrogen quantum dots (CNQDs), which can be used as an ORR catalyst with catalytic activity. However, the catalyst only demonstrated enhanced ORR performance by coupling Ag NPs with the CNQDs to develop a CNQD/Ag NP catalyst. Niu and co-workers [29] found that N-CDs/graphene oxide (GO) hybrids demonstrate remarkable ORR electrocatalytic activity owing to the N-doped GO and the unique nanostructure that forms of the graphene layers with N-CDs, which accelerates the reaction by modifying the electronic structure. Some CDs also could be prepared on a large scale with well controlled size, high dispersity and solubility, which exhibited enhanced electrocatalytic activity towards the ORR in an alkaline medium [30,31]. To our best knowledge, there are few reports on N-doped CDs/TMN as effective ORR electrocatalysts. Furthermore, the rational design and preparation of CDs-based ORR electrocatalysts is necessary to optimize the performance at the atomic-level.

The pyrrole (Py) is often used as a nitrogen source for N-doping carbon material prepared by high-temperature pyrolysis method. Especially, polypyrrole (PPy) could simultaneously act as nitrogen source and carbon source due to its C, N-containing heterocyclic molecular. Taking full account of the potential of N-doped CDs/TMN composites as efficient electrocatalysts, a sandwich-like electrode, which contains a N-doped carbon layer encapsulated with uniform CDs (N-CDs) and a sheet-like WN for both-side formation of N-CDs (denoted as N-CDs@WN), were developed using a facile two-step route and put forward to achieve the improved electrocatalytic performance in ORR. Electrochemical performances have been thoroughly evaluated. It was demonstrated that the N-CDs@WN nanohybrids exhibited excellent ORR properties. The nanostructure consists of an interconnected carbon framework composed of highly catalytically active N-containing CDs with hierarchical WN NSs that result in O2 accessibility and fast electron transfer, correlating to with the synergistic effect between N-CDs and WN. In addition, the catalyst also exhibits a significant tolerance to methanol crossover and demonstrates a long-term durability.
All chemicals were purchased from commercial suppliers (Chron Chemicals, China). The chemicals were of analytical grade and were used without further purification.

Synthesis of CDs: CDs were synthesized by a hydrothermal method [32]. 2.67 g of citric acid and 0.90 g of urea were dissolved in 30 mL of deionized water. The mixture was placed in a Teflon autoclave for 12 h at 180 °C. The resulting product was then centrifuged at 10,000 rpm and dialyzed against deionized water for 48 h to achieve yellow suspension. The CDs powder was collected after it was freeze-dried. Finally, ultra-sonication was used to disperse the synthesized CDs (0.45 g) in deionized water (60 mL) to obtain the CDs solution (0.75 g mL−1).

Synthesis of N-CDs: The pre-treated CDs solution (40 mL) and 4 mM pyrrole monomer was dissolved in 1 M HCl and stirred for 30 min, followed by the drop-wise addition of 20 µL of H2O2 (29%) at room temperature. After 12 h of in-situ polymerization, the product was then filtered, thoroughly washed and dried at 60 °C in a vacuum oven to obtain black powder, denoted as PPy-CDs. The PPy-CDs sample was heated in a furnace tube under Ar gas at a rate of 5 °C min−1 up to 700 °C for 3 h, and then naturally cooled to room temperature. The obtained sample was denoted as N-CDs.

Preparation of WN NSs: Pristine WN NSs were prepared according to the previously reported method [33]. Firstly, 3.5 mmol Na2WO4·2H2O and 1.75 mmol K2SO4 were dissolved in 40 mL of deionized water with stirring until a clear solution was formed. 3 M HCl solution was then added into the suspension until the formation of a yellow precipitate. The solution was placed in a 100 mL Teflon autoclave for 12 h at 180 °C. The yellow precipitate was filtered, thoroughly washed with deionized water and ethanol, and dried in a vacuum oven overnight to obtain the WO3 nanostructure. The resulting WO3 samples were calcinated at 700 °C for 3 h under NH3/Ar flow to achieve WN NSs.

Preparation of N-CDs@WN nanohybrids: A certain content of PPy-CDs was added into the Na2WO4·2H2O and K2SO4, followed with the above synthesis of WN through a modified hydrothermal method and the same nitridation process to achieve the desired sandwich-like hierarchical N-CDs@WN. In the case of optimization experiments, the N-CDs@WN hybrid with different mass ratios of PPy-CDs powders (5 wt%, 10 wt%, 15 wt%, and 20 wt%) were abbreviated as N-CDs-5@WN, N-CDs-10@WN, N-CDs-15@WN, and N-CDs-20@WN, respectively. In comparison, a CD + WN mixture was prepared by mechanically mixing the corresponding mass of CDs and WN.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) data were gathered using a powder diffractometer with a Cu Kα radiation source (D8 Advance A25X, Bruker Co., Germany). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed on a spectrometer equipped with a 165 mm hemispherical electron energy analyzer (Escalab 250Xi, Thermo Fisher Scientific Co., USA). Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) nitrogen adsorption–desorption on a gas adsorption analyser (Quantachrome Co., USA) was used to record specific surface areas. The morphological properties were determined using a scanning electron microscope (SEM, Inspect F50, FEI Co., USA) and a transmission electron microscope (TEM, Tecnai G2 F20, FEI Co., USA).

Electrochemical experiments were performed on a CHI 760E electrochemical workstation with a rotating disk electrode (RDE) apparatus at room temperature, using a standard three-electrode system. The counter electrode was a graphite plate and the reference electrode used was Ag/AgCl electrode. The working electrodes (glassy carbon, GC) were modified with a homogenous ink, prepared by ultrasonically suspending the catalyst (5 mg) for 30 min in a solution containing 1 mL of ethanol and 50 µL of Nafion® solution (5 wt%, Du Pont). The catalyst ink (20 µL) was placed on a GC electrode with a diameter of 5 mm and dried using an infrared lamp. A Pine electrochemical system was used to perform the RDE measurements. The system rotations ranged from 625 to 2025 rpm (revolutions per minute) with the scan rate of 10 mV s−1. The Koutecky–Levich (K–L) equation was used to determine the number of transferred electrons (n):
1 J
=
1
J k
+
1
J L
=
1
J k
+
1
B
ω
- 0.5
B = 0.2 n F
D
o 2
2 / 3
v
- 1 / 6
C
o 2
the variables include the apparent current density (J), kinetic current density (Jk ), limiting diffusion current density (JL ), and the RDE rotation rate (ω). B is determined from the slope of the plots and can be calculated using the equation above with Faraday’s constant (F = 96,485 C mol−1), the diffusion coefficient of O2 in 0.1 M KOH (D O2 = 1.9 × 10−5 cm2 s−1), the kinematic viscosity of 0.1 M KOH (ν = 0.01 cm2 s−1), and the bulk concentration of dissolved O2 (C O2 = 1.2 × 10−6 mol cm−3).

The synthesis process of N-CDs@WN nanohybrid is illustrated in Fig. 1 , which includes two steps. A typical synthesis of PPy-CDs was first prepared, and then the resulting mixture of PPy-CDs and tungsten-containing precursors was treated by a facile hydrothermal and nitridation process to produce sandwich-like N-CDs@WN. N-CDs serving as the “bridge” also easily attach to the WN nanosheets and fabricates the N-containing carbon layers on both sides of the WN. WN nanosheets were chosen as a “spacer” for the high temperature treatment, resulting in uniform distribution without agglomeration or stacking. In this sandwich-like structure, synergistic effect between WN and carbon, sheet-like morphology, and electronic conductivity may facilitate good contact with the electrolyte and hence achieve high ORR catalytic performances. As shown in Fig. 2 a, CDs exhibited a narrow size distribution around 5–6 nm. The structure and phase of as-prepared CDs, WN, and N-CDs@WN were determined using XRD, shown in Fig. 2b. The XRD pattern of the CDs distinctly exhibited a significant diffraction peak at around 27.2°, which was assigned to the C (0 0 2) plane and the less intense signals located around 43.7 and 54.3° were indexed to the C (1 0 0) and (0 0 4) plane, respectively. Generally, the diffraction peak at 26° corresponds to graphitized carbon, which exhibits an excellent electroconductivity due to its increased graphitization degree [28,34]. Note that the pattern of WN NSs displays the distinguishing diffraction peaks at 2θ values of 35.8, 48.5, 66.1, and 75.8°, which showed a hexagonal structure (JCPDS card no. 25-1256) [33]. The characteristic peaks for N-CDs@WN provide direct evidence for the existence of CDs and WN in the nanohybrid, indicating that the nanostructure consists of tungsten nitride nanosheets decorated with carbon dots. The crystalline structures of the CDs and N-CDs were also further investigated by Raman spectroscopy (Fig. 2c). The peaks around 1345 and 1580 cm−1 correspond to the D and G bands of the carbon nanostructures, respectively. The D band is related to the disordered sp2-hybridized carbon, whereas the G band is the result of the highly ordered graphite. The structural distortion in the N-CDs induced by the different bond lengths of CC and CN [35]. Compared with those of the CDs, the G band of the N-CDs shifts up to 1587 cm−1, indicating the successful doping of the N atoms. Meanwhile, the D/G intensity ratio (0.95) for the N-CDs is very close to that of the CDs (1.08), indicating the similar degree of graphitization. The Raman spectra of N-CDs@WN shows peaks around 260, 695 and 801 cm−1, which corresponds to the Raman peaks of WN and confirmed the successful fabrication of the WN (Fig. 2d) [36]. The peaks beyond 1000 cm−1 are attributed to the Raman peaks of the carbon.

The porous structure of nanocomposites was further investigated by the measurement of N2 adsorption–desorption. The pore size distribution and the nitrogen adsorption and desorption isotherm are presented in Fig. 3 . BET specific surface areas of CDs, WN, and N-CDs were 94.5, 106.34, and 197.0 m2 g−1, respectively. The corresponding specific surface area of N-CDs@WN was estimated to be 236.2 m2 g−1, which is about 2 times higher than those of pristine WN and CDs, resulting from sheet-like structure originated from WN NSs and effective crosslinker induced by CDs avoiding the agglomeration of bulk carbon [37]. The BJH distribution image reveals that the abundant porous structure lied in the range of 3–7 nm, which is favorable for the electrochemical performance due to the facile oxygen transfer process. This hierarchical porous structure is mainly ascribed to the important roles of the hybridized CDs and nanosheets, which could contribute to further preventing aggregation of CDs by WN “spacer”. TEM image in Fig. 4 a shows as-synthesized CDs with a diameter of 5–7 nm, which is in good agreement with the particle number distribution in Fig. 2a. The high resolution TEM (HRTEM) results (inset of Fig. 4a) reveal good crystallinity of the CDs with a well-defined 0.21 nm lattice spacing, representing the (1 0 0) facet of graphite [38]. TEM image of N-CDs (Fig. 4b), derived from PPy-CDs via a direct calcination, reveals the formation of a carbon layer encapsulated with uniform CDs. The lattice spacing was measured to be 0.335 nm, similar to the lattice spacing of the (0 0 2) plane of graphite [39]. For comparison, the SEM image in Fig. 4c depicts the pristine WN without the decoration of CDs in the self-assembled nanosheets. The TEM image of WN (Fig. 4d) exhibits the interconnected layers and smooth surfaces, demonstrating strong (1 0 0) preferred orientation as shown in XRD patterns of WN (Fig. 2b). It may act as a shape-directing template and facilitates the formation of the final sandwich-like structure. Fig. 4e further confirms the morphology transformation to a sandwich-like nanostructure. The N-CDs can be observed to be anchored on both sides of the WN NSs, which confirms that the sheet-like structure did not drastically change. Fig. 4f displays a detailed structure of the carbon layer with CDs anchored on the surface of WN, which can promote the transport of oxygen and provide enriched edge defects, all of which are advantageous for ORR. We speculate that the upper and lower porous layers could be beneficial for the rapid transport of oxygen and the N-doping in the porous carbon layers and WN guaranteeing fast electron transfer during the ORR process.

XPS spectra of the N-CDs@WN hybrids were collected and are shown in Fig. 5 . The overall XPS spectrum (Fig. 5a) reveals the chemical bonding states of W, C, N and O elements, corresponding to the peaks in 35.5, 284.5, 400.0 and 532.2 eV. Fig. 5b shows the high-resolution C1s spectrum, which allows for further assignment of three single peaks. The most prominent signal at 284.5 eV corresponds to the graphite-like carbon, indicating that the majority of C atoms exhibit a conjugated hexagonal lattice arrangement. The remaining two signals at 285.1 and 288.6 eV refer to the CN and CO bonds, respectively [40]. The W4f spectra (Fig. 5c) showed that W from WN was the predominate component for N-CDs@WN hybrid at 35.4 and 37.8 eV, which is in accordance with WN reported in literature [41]. The spectrum of N1s (Fig. 5d) can be summarized by four signals present at 398.5, 399.5, 400.6, and 402.0 eV, which are attributed to pyridinic N, pyrrolic N, quaternary N, and graphitic N, respectively [40,42,43]. The higher contents of pyrrolic and pyridinic N is due to the PPy acting as a nitrogen source and was incorporated into carbon atoms. Especially, nitrogen atoms were simultaneously incorporated into carbon layer derived from PPy itself and the inner carbon dots to cause the charge transfer.

To explore the roles of the CDs, WN NSs, N-CDs, and N-CDs@WN during the ORR electrochemical process, the ORR electrocatalytic activities of these catalysts were first tested through the CV measurement. All potentials are standardized using a reversible hydrogen electrode (RHE). Fig. S1 demonstrates that all the samples show obvious cathodic peaks in O2-saturated solution compared to those in the Ar-saturated electrolyte, signifying their intrinsic ORR activity [44]. In contrast to the inferior conductivity of CDs and WN, the as-prepared N-CDs and N-CDs@WN exhibit high conductivity with superior reduction current. The CVs of the N-CDs@WN hybrid exhibited a peak potential of 0.69 V and the greatest peak current of 310 μA, confirming its excellent catalytic activity for ORR. Linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) were also performed to investigate the ORR performance of the samples as-prepared. These experiments were conducted in a 0.1 M KOH solution that is O2-saturated at 1600 rpm with 10 mV s−1. The other N-CDs@WN hybrids, prepared with different nitridation temperatures (600 and 800 °C, abbreviated as N-CDs@WN-600 and N-CDs@WN-800), were also investigated by LSV in order to understand the temperature dependence of the ORR catalytic activity. As shown in Fig. S2a, the N-CDs@WN-700 presents the onset potential that is most positive with the greatest current density exhibiting the highest electrocatalytic activity for ORR. The improved electrocatalytic activity can be attributed to two factors. The first is its sandwich-like hierarchical structure, which produces more surface area, leading to more active sites compared to the N-CDs@WN-800, in which the structure was destroyed after the high temperature calcination. According to the XPS results (Fig. S3), peaks of C, N, W, N and O percentage were listed in Table S1. It demonstrates that the pyrolysis temperature is a critical factor in determining the chemical state and content of nitrogen atoms. By increasing the temperature of nitridation from 600 °C to 800 °C for the same 3 h duration, it was determined that the total N atomic% decreased. It was found that N-CDs@WN-700 contains more graphitic-N and pyridinic-N, which is favorable for enhancing the ORR efficiency. We can conclude the other reason is its moderate ratio of graphitic-N and pyridinic-N, which could enhance the electronic conductivity of the electrocatalyst and produce the increased active sites for the ORR electrocatalysis. In addition, the catalyst component was further optimized revealing that the highest ORR activity was found with a composite containing a moderate WN/PPy-CDs ratio, i.e. N-CDs@WN hybrid prepared with 10 wt% PPy-CDs (Fig. S4). The content value of 10% is the preferred condition, and the following catalyst were all prepared with this optimized ratio. It is speculated that a lower CDs content is deficient of active sites for ORR, while a higher CDs content may lower its dispersion and reduce the electrocatalytic activity.

LSVs of various catalysts is demonstrated in Fig. 6 a, that the CDs have the poorest ORR performance at the onset potentials (Eonset) of 0.75 V (vs. RHE), corresponding to the reduction of O2 to HO2 and further reduction of HO2 to OH [30]. Meanwhile, the pristine WN NSs and N-CDs also show lower Eonset and inferior limiting diffusion current density in comparison to N-CDs@WN, indicating the necessity of introducing the carbon layer to create pores and more exposed active sites. Consequently, the activity is further improved on N-CDs@WN with an Eonset at 0.95 V and a half wave potential (E1/2) of 0.78 V, which is similar to the Eonset (0.99 V) and E1/2 (0.83 V) of commercial Pt/C. The corresponding K–L plots obtained from N-CDs@WN are presented in Fig. 6b (inset). These plots suggest first-order reaction kinetics with respect to the dissolved O2 concentration in the electrolyte [45]. The electron transfer number was calculated per molecule of oxygen during the ORR process and was determined to be 3.8, suggesting that a nearly four-electron (4e−) process dominates the ORR in this hybrid. Having such a 4e− pathway is desirable and beneficial for fuels cells, which provides a faster oxygen reduction rate and high efficiency [42,46]. Previous researchers believed the pyridinic N sites are the prevalent active sites in the N-doped carbons which catalyze the 4-electron reduction process in the ORR [47–49]. The ORR kinetic current density (jk) was used for further comparison of the catalytic activities, as demonstrated in Fig. 6c. The jk value of N-CDs@WN hybrids was found to be 23.5 mA cm−2 at 0.30 V, which is approximately twice the value of the CD + WN mixture, 5 times that of CDs, and comparable to the value for Pt/C. The decrease on CD + WN mixture can be attributed to a slight aggregation of CDs was observed on the surface of WN, which result from the distribution of the CDs were affected by the supporting material (as shown in Fig. S5). Additionally, the calculated Tafel slopes were approximately 78 mV dec−1 for Pt/C, 79 mV dec−1 for N-CDs@WN, and 277 mV dec−1 for the CD + WN mixture (Fig. 6d) in the high overpotential region. It should be specified that the Tafel slope of the N-CDs@WN closest to Pt/C, which further indicates the high catalytic activity. To evaluate the electrocatalytic activity of N-CDs@WN as a potential catalyst for ORR, the normalized properties are compared to previously reported carbon dots-based catalysts (Table 1 ). It is evident the sandwich-like N-CDs@WN demonstrates higher catalytic activity comparatively, which is attributed to the specific nanostructure features and the synergistic effect of WN and CDs.

Moreover, to explore the durability of N-CDs@WN in alkaline solutions, the chronoamperometric was performed in a 0.1 M KOH solution with a potential of 0.7 V (Fig. 7 a). N-CDs@WN exhibited superior stability, which was a crucial factor for the practical application for fuel cells. After 20,000 s of continuous ORR process, the current density retained ∼82.3% of its original ORR performance, meanwhile the performance of the commercial Pt/C dropped to 73.6%. The tolerance of N-CDs@WN and Pt/C catalysts to methanol is demonstrated in Fig. 7b. A significant decrease in ORR activity was evident for Pt/C as methanol was introduced to the system. Meanwhile, the ORR activity for N-CDs@WN remained unchanged. Due to the sufficient durability, exceptional tolerance to methanol and higher ORR activity, N-CDs@WN appears to be a hopeful substitute for Pt/C in alkaline DMFCs.

Based on these results, it is a reasonable conclusion that the enhanced ORR activity of N-CDs@WN primarily stems from the synergistic effects between N-CDs and WN, which were to become ORR active sites, and to contribute a significant surface area and a facile electron pathway to WN, respectively. By introducing N-CDs to WN, there was little influence on the properties and morphology of WN. However, the aggregation of NPs was hindered, but the ORR activity is enhanced significantly by two major contributing factors: (I) Large mesopores are formed due to the development of a sandwich-like structure produced by 0D CDs and 2D WN nanosheets, resulting in the active sites being easily accessed by the electrolyte. (II) Electron transfer to the surface of WN is facilitated by the incredibly conductive electron pathway contributed by the hybrid material.

A facile and straightforward method was developed to synthesize sandwich-like carbon-anchored 2D nanosheets, which is composed of CDs and N-rich carbon layer decorated on WN NSs with improved electrocatalytic performance in ORR. The N-CDs@WN electrode was determined to have an electron transfer number of ca. 3.8, suggesting that the prevailing mechanism is a favorable 4e-reduction pathway. Therefore, demonstrating a desirable long-term stability and resistance to methanol. The sandwich-like structure assembled between 0D CDs and 2D nanosheets not only provides active sites for ORR and promotes dispersion of homogeneous CD particles, but also benefits for the increase of electrochemically active surface area. For another, the excellent ORR performance may be ascribed to the synergistic effect occurring between N-CDs and WN involved make the sandwich-like hybrid more active and efficient for electrochemical behaviors. These results can provide new insights in designing and developing various metal-free, highly efficient ORR catalysts, which are necessary in developing practical applications in fuel cells.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work was supported by the Key Research and Development Projects in Sichuan Province (2017GZ0397), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 51602209), the Provincial Nature Science Foundation of Sichuan (Nos. 2016GZ0423, 2017CC0017, 2018FZ0105) and the Fundamental Research Funds supported by Ministry of Education of the People’s Republic of China (No. YJ201746, 2018SCUH0025). In addition, Jie Zhang would wish to give her sincere thanks to Nako Yabuki and Jennie Tang for their great support and tremendous encouragement.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apsusc.2018.10.116.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
